author: decentralizedlaw
content:
Thanks! Glad you appreciate the idea...

The references don't fragment because a citation never points at "the law" as a moving target. It points at an immutable version recorded in a law-registry on Ethereum, including (registry, chain, category, document, version) and a content hash. Amendments are append-only: a new version is added to the registry with a relation back to the old one, and "what's in force now" is derived from the reference graph at read time. The old citation keeps resolving to exactly the bytes it always did, forever. That's the beauty of using the blockchain for it.

Jurisdictional differences exist, but not different from any online activity. The way this is dealt with is 1) by electing a governing law and court as part of the legal stack, 2) by focusing on areas of law where contracts and private standards already dominate human interaction, instead of big idea conflicting with legal reality like Network States, or things that are highly local/cultural, like family law.

Examples of areas dominated by private standards are the current financial system, which basically runs on agreements;  international trade; and blockchain transactions, of course.

Some of the largest financial markets, like derivatives, are governed by private standards (ISDA Master Agreement).
